flashcards.back: Programmed I/O is a method where the CPU directly controls the data transfer process between devices.
Interrupt-driven I/O is a method where the CPU is notified by an interrupt signal when a device is ready for data transfer.
DMA (Direct Memory Access) is a method where a device can directly access memory without CPU intervention.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of programmed I/O?
flashcards.back: The main advantage of programmed I/O is its simplicity and low cost.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of programmed I/O?
flashcards.back: The main disadvantage of programmed I/O is that it can be slow and inefficient.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of interrupt-driven I/O?
flashcards.back: The main advantage of interrupt-driven I/O is that it allows for efficient use of CPU time.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of interrupt-driven I/O?
flashcards.back: The main disadvantage of interrupt-driven I/O is that it can be complex to implement.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of DMA?
flashcards.back: The main advantage of DMA is that it allows for fast and efficient data transfer.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of DMA?
flashcards.back: The main disadvantage of DMA is that it can be complex to implement and requires specialized hardware.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the CPU in programmed I/O?
flashcards.back: The CPU directly controls the data transfer process in programmed I/O.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the CPU in interrupt-driven I/O?
flashcards.back: The CPU is notified by an interrupt signal and then handles the data transfer in interrupt-driven I/O.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the CPU in DMA?
flashcards.back: The CPU is not directly involved in the data transfer process in DMA.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between programmed I/O and interrupt-driven I/O?
flashcards.back: Programmed I/O involves the CPU directly controlling the data transfer process, while interrupt-driven I/O involves the CPU being notified by an interrupt signal.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between interrupt-driven I/O and DMA?
flashcards.back: Interrupt-driven I/O involves the CPU handling the data transfer, while DMA involves the device directly accessing memory without CPU intervention.

flashcards.front: What is the main application of programmed I/O?
flashcards.back: Programmed I/O is commonly used in simple embedded systems and low-speed devices.
